title: Magnetic fields due to currents
summary: Cambridge 9702 Paper 4 — Magnetic fields due to currents (20.4). Senpai Corner diagram-backed pilot with premium structure and live visuals.
learningObjectives: Describe the magnetic field patterns produced by currents in straight wires and solenoids.
Apply the Right-Hand Grip Rule to determine magnetic field direction.
Calculate magnetic field strengths and forces between parallel wires using relevant formulae.

simpleExplanation.title: Currents: Magnets on the Move
simpleExplanation.summary: It might seem surprising, but any moving electric charge, like an electric current flowing through a wire, naturally creates a magnetic field around it. This lesson explores how these magnetic fields look, how strong they are, and how they can even exert forces on other current-carrying wires.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine a fast-flowing river. The main current is like the electric current in a wire. The ripples and eddies that spread outwards and influence everything near the riverbanks are like the magnetic field created by the current, invisibly affecting its surroundings.
steps: Visualise electric current as the fundamental source of a magnetic field.
Use the Right-Hand Grip Rule to determine the direction of the magnetic field around a straight current-carrying wire.
Understand how the direction of current affects the attractive or repulsive forces between parallel wires.
Recognise the characteristics of the strong, uniform magnetic field found inside a current-carrying solenoid.

flashcards.front: How do electric currents create magnetic fields?
flashcards.back: Electric currents, which are moving electric charges, inherently generate magnetic fields around them.

flashcards.front: What rule determines the direction of the magnetic field around a straight current-carrying wire?
flashcards.back: The Right-Hand Grip Rule (or Right-Hand Rule).

flashcards.front: What is the formula for the force per unit length between two parallel wires carrying currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ separated by distance $r$?
How does inserting an iron core affect a solenoid's magnetic field?
flashcards.back: The magnetic field strength inside the solenoid significantly increases.

flashcards.front: Describe the magnetic field inside a current-carrying solenoid.
flashcards.back: It is strong, uniform, and its pattern closely resembles that of a bar magnet.

flashcards.front: What does $\mu_0$ represent in magnetic field formulae?
flashcards.back: It's the permeability of free space, a constant that quantifies how easily a magnetic field can pass through a vacuum.

flashcards.front: What is the magnetic field pattern around a straight current-carrying wire?
flashcards.back: It consists of concentric circles centred on the wire, with the field strength decreasing with distance.

flashcards.front: Under what condition do two parallel current-carrying wires attract each other?
flashcards.back: When they carry electric currents flowing in the same direction.

flashcards.front: How do you determine the North pole of a solenoid?
flashcards.back: If, when viewed from an end, the current appears to flow anti-clockwise, that end is the North pole.

flashcards.front: What is the formula for the magnetic field strength $B$ at distance $r$ from a long straight wire?

sections.content: Prepare to unlock one of physics' most fascinating secrets: the connection between **electricity** and **magnetism**. You'll discover how electric currents aren't just flows of charge, but also powerful sources that generate their own **magnetic fields**. This lesson will equip you with the tools to predict these fields' directions and strengths, from simple wires to complex coils, fundamental knowledge for your Cambridge Physics exams. This pilot aligns with **Senpai Corner** A Level notes (2025–2027) for topic **20.4**.

When an electric current flows through a straight conductor, it generates a magnetic field in the space around it. This field isn't random; its pattern forms distinct concentric circles that encircle the wire. The field strength is strongest closest to the wire and diminishes as you move further away. The specific direction of these circular magnetic field lines is crucial and can be found using a simple hand rule.

items: Magnetic field patterns are formed wherever a current is flowing.
An electromagnet is a temporary magnet made by winding an insulated wire around a soft iron core, forming a coil known as a solenoid.
When current passes through the solenoid it produces a magnetic field.
The solenoid is said to magnetised and becomes an electromagnet.

sections.content: The **Right-Hand Grip Rule** is your key to understanding field direction. Imagine grasping the wire with your right hand: if your thumb points in the direction of the **conventional current** (positive to negative), then your curled fingers will indicate the direction of the **magnetic field lines** around the wire. This rule is essential for correctly interpreting magnetic phenomena.

The strength of this magnetic field (B) at a distance 'r' from a long straight wire is given by: $$B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$$ Here, I is the current, and $\mu_0$ is the **permeability of free space** ($4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$), a constant describing how magnetic fields interact with a vacuum.

Because each current-carrying wire produces its own magnetic field, two parallel wires carrying currents will exert magnetic forces on each other. This interaction is a direct consequence of one wire's current experiencing a force from the other wire's magnetic field. The direction of this force depends entirely on the relative directions of the currents.

items: Two parallel current-carrying wires exert forces on each other.
If currents are in the same direction, the wires attract.
If currents are in opposite directions, the wires repel.

sections.content: The force per unit length ($\frac{F}{L}$) between two long, parallel current-carrying wires separated by a distance 'r' is: $$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi r}$$ Where $I_1$ and $I_2$ are the currents in the two wires.
Magnetic Fields from Coils and Solenoids

A single loop of current-carrying wire also generates a magnetic field, most intense at its centre. However, for a powerful and controlled magnetic field, we turn to **solenoids**. A solenoid is essentially a long coil of wire, tightly wound, which produces a remarkably strong and uniform magnetic field within its interior when current flows through it.

items: A single current loop creates a field strongest at its centre.
A solenoid produces a strong, uniform magnetic field inside.
The solenoid's field pattern resembles a bar magnet's.
Solenoid polarity (N/S) depends on current direction view.
Clockwise current (from an end) means that end is a South pole.
Anti-clockwise current (from an end) means that end is a North pole.
Adding a soft iron core greatly increases a solenoid's field strength.

sections.question: Two long, parallel wires are separated by a distance of 0.15 m. Wire 1 carries a current of 3.0 A, and Wire 2 carries a current of 5.0 A in the same direction. Calculate the force per unit length between the wires. ($\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$)
sections.solution:
1.  **Identify known values:**
    Current $I_1 = 3.0 \text{ A}$
    Current $I_2 = 5.0 \text{ A}$
    Separation $r = 0.15 \text{ m}$
    Permeability of free space $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$
2.  **Choose the correct formula:**
    The formula for force per unit length between parallel wires is: $$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi r}$$ 
3.  **Substitute the values into the formula:**
    $$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (3.0) \times (5.0)}{2\pi \times (0.15)}$$
4.  **Perform the calculation:**
    $$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{60\pi \times 10^{-7}}{0.3\pi} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-7}}{0.3} = 200 \times 10^{-7} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N m}^{-1}$$
5.  **State the direction of the force:**
    Since the currents are in the same direction, the force between the wires is **attractive**.

    **Answer:** The force per unit length between the wires is $2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N m}^{-1}$ (attractive).

sections.content: Always remember to apply the **Right-Hand Grip Rule** consistently for both straight wires and solenoids (to determine polarity). Double-check the direction of current for parallel wires; 'same direction' means attraction, 'opposite direction' means repulsion – this is a common point for losing marks if confused!

items.label: Senpai Corner A Level Physics notes (source)

faq.q: How does a current-carrying wire produce a magnetic field?
faq.a: A current-carrying wire produces a magnetic field because electric current is a flow of charged particles (electrons). These moving charges inherently generate a magnetic field in the space surrounding their path, as described by the fundamental principles of electromagnetism.

faq.q: Why is a solenoid's magnetic field uniform inside?
faq.a: Inside a long solenoid, the magnetic fields from each individual turn of the coil add up, effectively cancelling out any transverse components and reinforcing the longitudinal components. This results in nearly parallel, equally spaced magnetic field lines, indicating a strong and remarkably uniform magnetic field within its core.

faq.q: What is the significance of the permeability of free space ($\mu_0$)?
faq.a: The permeability of free space ($\mu_0$) is a fundamental physical constant that quantifies the ability of a vacuum to permit the formation of a magnetic field within it. It acts as a proportionality constant in equations relating magnetic fields to electric currents, indicating how readily a magnetic field can 'pass through' or be established in empty space.
